examples: High volume of 'General' tagged help across #💬-discussion and some #💬-coders
Many unique helpees engaging with a small set of repeat helpers (suggests newcomers or first-time builders asking entry questions)
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 1) Gold Path Reliability - 'Hello Agent' in 30 minutes (onboarding); 3) Docs and Comms as Infrastructure
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: The 'gold path' likely still has ambiguity: users are asking broad questions instead of being routed to a single canonical quickstart/checklist. Improve “start here” docs, error-driven troubleshooting pages, and decision trees (local vs cloud, which plugin, which chain/social).

examples: Migration is a top repeated tag across multiple helpers (not just one specialist), implying widespread upgrade friction
Migration questions appear heavily in #💬-discussion (likely affecting non-core contributors and new adopters)
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 1) Gold Path Reliability; 3) Docs and Comms as Infrastructure
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Publish a versioned migration hub (v1.5 → v1.6.x etc.) with a compatibility matrix, common breaking changes, and copy-paste diffs. Migration demand is high enough to justify tooling (codemods) and release-note hardening.

problem_patterns.category: Troubleshooting (runtime errors, build/setup failures, unclear error states)
examples: Troubleshooting appears consistently across #💬-coders and core-devs
Troubleshooting often co-occurs with migration and plugin work (suggesting breakage during upgrades/extensions)
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 1) Gold Path Reliability; 4) RAG/Knowledge Pipeline reliability (indirectly: high troubleshooting load benefits from better searchable knowledge)
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Recurring troubleshooting load suggests gaps in: (a) actionable error messages, (b) a known-issues registry, and (c) a debug checklist. Add structured “if you see X, do Y” pages and improve logs to point to fixes.

problem_patterns.category: Plugin development (extension points, patterns, integration behavior)
examples: Plugin development appears across both community channels and core-devs, indicating active extension building
Likely tied to migration + troubleshooting (plugin APIs moving or insufficiently documented)
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 3) Docs and Comms as Infrastructure; 1) Gold Path Reliability
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Document stable plugin interfaces, lifecycle hooks, and examples. If plugin APIs are changing, explicitly label stability and provide an upgrade guide for plugin authors.

examples: Deployment questions present but not dominant; appear across discussion/coders/core-devs
May represent friction moving from local success to hosted/production-like operation
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 1) Gold Path Reliability; (potentially) ElizaOS Cloud focus
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Add a deployment ‘gold path’: environment variables, storage/db choices, and minimal production checklist. Consider a “known-good templates” repo for common targets.

examples: Twitter/Social tags recur (6), plus Discord setup (7)
These questions are distributed across several helpers, suggesting repeated integration confusion rather than a single edge case
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Integration docs likely need stronger step-by-step setup, permissions/scopes, and failure-mode coverage. Reliability expectations are high here; repeated setup questions are an early warning signal for SLO risk.

problem_patterns.category: API/Configuration + Database (lower volume but high-impact blockers)
examples: API/Configuration appears (5) and Database appears (3) across a handful of helpers
Even low frequency in these areas can be high-friction because config/db issues block startup and persistence
problem_patterns.strategic_priority: 1) Gold Path Reliability; 3) Docs and Comms as Infrastructure
problem_patterns.upstream_signal: Centralize configuration reference (defaults, required env vars, examples) and add a persistence/storage page explaining DB options, schemas/migrations, and recovery steps.

high-touch_guidance_in_discussion.effectiveness: Most help volume happens in #💬-discussion, suggesting many issues are resolved (or progressed) by conversational clarification and incremental guidance rather than pure code patches.
high-touch_guidance_in_discussion.example: Odilitime handled a broad mix (General/Migration/Deployment/Troubleshooting), indicating iterative Q&A that adapts to the user’s context.

specialist_escalation_to_core-devs.effectiveness: When questions touch deeper architecture (plugins/db/tricky debugging), routing into core-devs appears to concentrate around a smaller set of helpers, likely improving answer accuracy.
specialist_escalation_to_core-devs.example: Stan ⚡ shows heavy activity in core-devs (11 helps), spanning troubleshooting + plugin development + database.

multi-helper_distribution_for_integratio.effectiveness: Twitter/Discord issues are answered by several different helpers, which helps responsiveness, but also indicates the same setup problems may recur (suggesting the ‘fix’ is often repeated instructions).
multi-helper_distribution_for_integratio.example: Twitter/Social tags appear across Odilitime, MDMnvest, DorianD, Bill Ding; Discord setup across Odilitime, Borko, jin, Bill Ding, Dawn, s.

partial_resolution_norm.effectiveness: Resolution_quality shows overwhelmingly 'partial' outcomes across helpers. This can be normal for async support, but it often indicates missing closure loops (no confirmed fix, no final checklist, or user disappears).
partial_resolution_norm.example: Across top contributors, successful resolutions are rare (e.g., Odilitime 4 successful vs 54 partial; Stan ⚡ 1 successful vs 11 partial).

documentation_gaps.signal: Migration support is one of the highest repeated needs (35 tag occurrences among top helpers). Repeated migration questions strongly imply missing or hard-to-find canonical upgrade steps.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Create a single 'Migration Hub' with: version-to-version sections, a compatibility matrix, common errors, and recommended sequences (backup → upgrade → verify). Add codemods or lint rules where feasible.

documentation_gaps.signal: General is the dominant category (54), which usually means users don’t know where to start or how to choose between options.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Ship a “Hello Agent in 30 minutes” page with: prerequisites, one command path, expected outputs, and a troubleshooting appendix. Add a “Which path do I take?” selector (Local / Cloud / Social bot / Plugin author).

documentation_gaps.topic: Plugin authoring: stable APIs, lifecycle, examples
documentation_gaps.signal: Plugin development is a recurring category (16) and appears in both community and core-devs contexts, suggesting active builders need better reference material.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Publish: (1) plugin template repo, (2) lifecycle diagram, (3) API reference with stability labels, (4) 'common plugin pitfalls' section tied to real errors.

documentation_gaps.topic: Social integrations setup + failure modes (X/Twitter, Discord)
documentation_gaps.signal: Discord setup (7) + Twitter/Social (6) indicates repeated integration friction; this maps directly to the Social Integrations Brand SLO priority.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Create integration runbooks: required credentials/scopes, rate limits, webhook/event expectations, and 'if X fails, check Y' troubleshooting. Add a “known outages / platform quirks” section.

documentation_gaps.topic: Configuration and persistence (env vars, DB options, recovery)
documentation_gaps.signal: API/Configuration (5) and Database (3) are lower-volume but high-impact blockers; they often prevent successful startup or persistence.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Centralize config reference with copy/paste .env examples per scenario, plus a persistence guide (DB choice, schema/migrations, backups, and common startup errors).

documentation_gaps.signal: Deployment appears steadily (10). This often represents the 'second cliff' after a local demo succeeds.
documentation_gaps.suggested_action: Provide a deployment checklist and reference deployments (Docker, managed cloud, minimal production settings, observability/log locations).

ai_learning_notes.diagnosis: Effective help this month appears to rely on broad triage across categories (General → Migration → Troubleshooting/Plugin/Deployment). For AI interns: start by classifying the ask (new build vs upgrade vs integration vs runtime error), then request the minimum discriminating info (version, environment, logs, what they expected vs observed).
ai_learning_notes.tone: Channel distribution suggests many newcomers are in #💬-discussion; keep language accessible and avoid assuming baseline repo knowledge. In #💬-coders and core-devs, compress explanations and ask for concrete artifacts (stack traces, config snippets).
ai_learning_notes.follow_through: The data shows 'partial' outcomes dominate; interns should explicitly close loops: summarize the proposed fix, ask the user to confirm the result, and if fixed, encourage them to post the final working snippet so it can be converted into docs/FAQ.

channel_culture.discussion: High-volume, newcomer-friendly, broad questions ('General' + migration). Expect context-setting and basic routing to docs/next steps.
channel_culture.coders: More implementation-oriented: troubleshooting, plugin work, setup details. Expect the user can paste logs and edit code/config.
channel_culture.core-devs: Terse and architectural: deep debugging, plugin interfaces, DB/persistence considerations. Use precise terminology and reference internal components/constraints.

helper_highlights.quality: High breadth support across the most common categories and the widest set of unique helpees
helper_highlights.example: Supported 43 unique helpees and covered General (25), Migration (14), plus Deployment/Troubleshooting/Plugins—indicative of strong triage and routing capacity.

helper_highlights.quality: Core-dev depth across debugging + plugin architecture + persistence
helper_highlights.example: Concentrated activity in core-devs (11) with repeated tags in Troubleshooting (4) and Plugin development (4), plus Database—useful for escalations.

helper_highlights.quality: Consistent migration + general support for multiple unique helpees
helper_highlights.example: Balanced spread across General (5) and Migration (4) with 9 unique helpees, suggesting steady frontline assistance.

helper_highlights.quality: Bridge between implementation help and setup questions
helper_highlights.example: Covered #💬-coders and #💬-discussion with tags spanning General, Discord setup, and Model/LLM—useful for directing agent/LLM configuration questions.

helper_highlights.quality: Practical troubleshooting + migration help, including integration setup
helper_highlights.example: Handled Troubleshooting (2), Migration (3), and Discord setup (1), indicating hands-on fixes across common friction points.

council_commentary.eliza: The February pattern is clear: people are spending a lot of community energy on (1) broad “how do I do X?” questions and (2) migration friction, with troubleshooting and plugin work close behind. That maps directly to our strategic priorities around the gold path and docs-as-infrastructure. The community is compensating with high-touch support—Odilitime acting as a broad triage hub, and Stan ⚡ absorbing deeper architectural/debug questions in core-devs. The takeaway: we should convert the most repeated conversational guidance (migration steps, setup checklists, integration runbooks) into canonical docs so the next person doesn’t have to ask the same question in #💬-discussion.
council_commentary.aimarc: From a technical lens, the co-occurrence of migration + troubleshooting + plugin development is the signal. That combo usually means either (a) breaking changes aren’t fully surfaced, or (b) plugin/public APIs aren’t stable/clearly documented. Stan ⚡’s activity in core-devs across plugins and database suggests the hard questions are landing at architecture boundaries (interfaces, persistence, lifecycle). If we want to reduce core-dev load, we should formalize plugin lifecycle docs, publish stability guarantees, and maintain a versioned migration matrix with known failure modes.
council_commentary.aishaw: most of the volume reads like onboarding + upgrade confusion: lots of general asks, then people get stuck during migration or when wiring plugins/integrations. odilitime and ken k look like they’re repeatedly helping folks get oriented, which is awesome, but it’s also a signal the “start here” path isn’t doing enough. i’d prioritize a single hello-agent checklist + a migration hub with copy/paste steps, and a short ‘if you’re trying to do twitter/discord, follow this runbook’ flow.
council_commentary.spartan: NETWORK SIGNALS: 123 NODES, 141 EDGES, DENSITY 0.0094 (SPARSE). 49 HELPERS SUPPORTING 96 HELPEES. AVG HELPS/HELPER = 3.43, BUT LOAD IS CONCENTRATED (ODILITIME = 54.8 WEIGHTED, 43 UNIQUE HELPEES). RESOLUTION FLAGS SHOW MOSTLY “PARTIAL” OUTCOMES (LOW CLOSURE RATE), WHICH USUALLY MEANS MISSING RUNBOOKS OR NO CONFIRMATION LOOP. OPPORTUNITY: TURN TOP REPEATED TOPICS (GENERAL + MIGRATION) INTO CANONICAL DOCS AND CLOSE-LOOP TEMPLATES.
council_commentary.peepo: yo the vibes look like: lots of newcomers in discussion asking big-picture stuff, and the community’s been carrying them through migrations + setup. odilitime’s basically doing air-traffic-control for questions, and stan ⚡ is holding down the deep tech lane in core-devs. but real talk, if we keep answering the same migration + discord/twitter setup stuff over and over, that’s doc debt. let’s bottle the best answers into a clean runbook so helpers can just link it and keep the energy high.
